item.title: Black peppercorns
item.description: The unripe drupes of Piper nigrum, dried whole. Heat is piperine (fat-soluble); aroma is a stack of volatile terpenes that vanish within minutes of grinding.

item.title: Butter (unsalted)
item.description: Cow's-milk fat with ~16% water and ~2% milk solids. The solids are the flavor lever; the water and the melt curve are why it behaves so differently in different roles.

item.title: Chinkiang black vinegar (镇江香醋)
item.description: Aged glutinous-rice vinegar from Zhenjiang. Dark, malty, smoky, and only mildly acidic; closer in spirit to balsamic than to white vinegar.

item.title: Eggs (large)
item.description: Roughly 50 g per large US egg. Whites set at 62 °C, yolks at 70 °C, and the gap between those temperatures is half of what cooks do with eggs.

item.title: Evaporated milk
item.description: Cow's milk reduced by ~60% with no added sugar, then sterilized in the sealed can. Concentrated dairy body plus the faint Maillard depth that in-can heat builds.

item.title: Garlic
item.description: Allium sativum. Pungency comes from allicin, a sulfur compound that only forms when cells are damaged. How you cut a clove decides how it tastes.

item.title: Sui mi ya cai (碎米芽菜)
item.description: Salt-cured, sun-dried, long-fermented and minced stems of a mustard-green cultivar from Sichuan. A dry, dark, glutamate-dense seasoning paste in pickle form.

item.title: Sweetened condensed milk
item.description: Cow's milk reduced by ~60% with ~45% added sugar. The sugar is structural: it shelf-stabilizes the can by osmotic pressure and binds the protein-fat-water emulsion together.

item.title: Whole milk
item.description: Cow's milk at ~3.25% fat. Fat carries fat-soluble flavor; casein binds tannins and acid; lactose drives Maillard browning under heat.

item.title: Wood ear mushroom (木耳)
item.description: Dehydrated Auricularia heimuer fungus. Nearly flavorless, structurally chitinous, and bought primarily for the gel-and-crunch texture that survives long cooking.

item.title: French
item.description: Technique as the transferable unit. The French canon is less about ingredients than about a vocabulary of methods: emulsification, reduction, the roux. These travel across the full range of the cuisine.

item.title: Indian
item.description: Spice as a layered system, not a heat dial. Each spice enters at a specific moment (bloomed in fat, ground dry, added raw at the end) because the volatile compounds respond differently to heat at different stages.

item.title: Pantry makes
item.description: Store-bought replacements that are not actually hard to make and taste meaningfully different. One session sets you up for months.

item.title: Coconut milk (canned, full-fat)
item.description: Pressed coconut emulsion, ~17–22% fat. The lipid phase that carries fat-soluble aromatics in Thai and Southeast Asian curries.

item.title: Dijon mustard
item.description: Brown and yellow mustard seeds soaked in white wine, ground smooth, finished with cider vinegar and salt. Sharp, hot, and creamy in a way no jar ever is.

item.title: Green curry pork
item.description: A coconut-based green curry augmented from store-bought paste with fresh lemongrass, ginger, and shrimp paste. Chinese eggplant absorbs the curry liquor; bamboo and shimeji carry the texture. Two cans of full-fat coconut milk give the dish enough lipid phase to braise without diluting with stock.

item.title: Homemade panko breadcrumbs
item.description: Crustless white bread, dried low and slow, then ground coarse. The result is the long, splintery shard of real panko, not the grainy dust of the supermarket can.

item.title: Sichuan chili oil
item.description: Aromatics infused into hot oil at three precise temperatures, then poured over coarse chili flakes. The crackle is the technique. The result has structure store-bought never does — three layers of red, with sediment that earns its bowl.

item.title: Toasted sesame paste (zhī ma jiàng)
item.description: Hulled white sesame seeds toasted dark, ground to a thick caramel-brown paste with a splash of toasted sesame oil. The Chinese pantry version: deeply roasted, structurally distinct from raw Levantine tahini.

item.title: Whole-grain mustard
item.description: Same Dijon chemistry, but pulsed coarse — the seeds stay intact and pop between the teeth, releasing heat in waves rather than all at once.

item.title: American
item.description: Layered fat with restrained sweetness in bakes and bright finishes (lemon, mustard, vinegar) in savory dishes. The throughline is dialing back what factory food overdoes.

item.title: Chinese
item.description: A four-axis pantry (soy, vinegar, aromatic, heat) combined in shifting ratios across regions. Every dish reads the cook's hand on those four levers.

item.title: Italian
item.description: Restraint plus fat: olive oil, cured pork, aged cheese, acid. The technique is in the ratios, not the ingredient count.

item.title: Thai
item.description: Balance across four registers (sweet, salt, heat, sour) held within a single dish, not separated across courses. The Thai catalog here is drinks, for now, and they teach extraction.

item.title: Digital Scale
item.description: Weighs ingredients in grams. The single most consequential tool for repeatable baking and accurate recipe scaling.

item.title: Dutch Oven
item.description: 5–7 quart enameled cast iron pot with a heavy lid. Mass for steady braising, seal for moisture, depth for stocks — and stovetop-to-oven without a transfer.

item.title: Heavy Saucepan
item.description: 2–3 quart pan with a thick conductive base. Thermal mass is the feature: stable low heat for tea, custards, and reductions that won't scorch.

item.title: Instant-Read Thermometer
item.description: Probe thermometer that reads internal temperature in 1–3 seconds. Replaces guesswork with a number on protein doneness, custards, brown butter, and oil.

item.title: Microplane
item.description: Photo-etched fine grater. Turns hard cheese, citrus zest, garlic, and ginger into a fluffy, dissolvable powder.

item.title: Wide Skillet
item.description: 12-inch sauté pan with sloped sides. The width is the spec: surface area drives evaporation, browning, and the room you need to finish pasta in the pan.
